content.thinking:
Now I have a clear picture:

1. CockroachDB is running with:
   - `crdb-cluster` (headless) service: ports 26257 (SQL) and 8080 (DB Console/HTTP)
   - `crdb-cluster-public` (ClusterIP) service: ports 26257 (SQL) and 8080 (DB Console/HTTP)

2. ingress-nginx is running with:
   - Controller pod running
   - LoadBalancer service (pending external IP - this is expected in a kind cluster)
   - Empty ConfigMap

For the DB Console at https://crdb-ui.example.com, I need to create a self-signed TLS certificate and an Ingress resource pointing to the DB Console service on port 8080. Since CockroachDB's HTTP console runs over HTTPS by default when TLS is configured, I'll need to handle the backend protocol appropriately.

For SQL access on port 26257, I need to set up TCP passthrough in the ingress-nginx controller by configuring a TCP services ConfigMap and ensuring the controller is launched with the proper flag to reference it.

content.thinking:
CockroachDB is running in **insecure mode** (the `--insecure` flag worked). So:
- The DB Console is served over plain HTTP on port 8080
- SQL is plain TCP on port 26257

The ingress-nginx controller does NOT currently have `--tcp-services-configmap` in its args. I need to:

1. Create a TCP services ConfigMap
2. Patch the ingress-nginx controller deployment to add `--tcp-services-configmap=ingress-nginx/tcp-services`
3. Patch the ingress-nginx controller service to expose port 26257
4. Create a self-signed TLS certificate for crdb-ui.example.com to handle HTTPS termination at the ingress level, since the backend is running insecure HTTP
5. Set up an Ingress resource for the DB Console that uses this certificate

I need to patch the ingress-nginx controller deployment to add the `--tcp-services-configmap` flag, then create a ConfigMap to route TCP traffic on port 26257 to the CockroachDB service. I'll also need to patch the ingress-nginx service to expose port 26257. Let me work through these steps systematically, starting with the TCP services ConfigMap and the necessary patches. Writing TLS and Ingress config...

content.text:
CockroachDB is running in insecure mode (HTTP on 8080). The ingress-nginx controller is missing `--tcp-services-configmap`. I need to:

1. Create a TCP services ConfigMap and patch the controller to use it
2. Expose port 26257 on the ingress-nginx service
3. Generate a TLS cert and create an Ingress for the DB Console

Let me do all of this:

input.command:
kubectl -n ingress-nginx create configmap tcp-services \
  --from-literal="26257"="cockroachdb/crdb-cluster-public:26257" \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f - 2>&1
input.description: Create TCP services ConfigMap for port 26257 routing

content.content: configmap/tcp-services created

tool_use_result.stdout: configmap/tcp-services created

input.command:
kubectl -n ingress-nginx patch deployment ingress-nginx-controller \
  --type=json \
  -p='[{"op":"add","path":"/spec/template/spec/containers/0/args/-","value":"--tcp-services-configmap=ingress-nginx/tcp-services"}]' 2>&1
input.description: Patch ingress-nginx controller deployment to enable TCP services ConfigMap

content.content: deployment.apps/ingress-nginx-controller patched

tool_use_result.stdout: deployment.apps/ingress-nginx-controller patched

input.command:
kubectl -n ingress-nginx patch svc ingress-nginx-controller \
  --type=json \
  -p='[{"op":"add","path":"/spec/ports/-","value":{"name":"crdb-sql","port":26257,"protocol":"TCP","targetPort":26257}}]' 2>&1
input.description: Add port 26257 to ingress-nginx controller service

content.content: service/ingress-nginx-controller patched

tool_use_result.stdout: service/ingress-nginx-controller patched

content.text: Now generate the self-signed TLS cert and create the DB Console ingress:

input.command:
openssl req -x509 -nodes -days 365 -newkey rsa:2048 \
  -keyout /tmp/crdb-ui.key -out /tmp/crdb-ui.crt \
  -subj "/CN=crdb-ui.example.com/O=crdb" \
  -addext "subjectAltName=DNS:crdb-ui.example.com" 2>&1
input.description: Generate self-signed TLS certificate for crdb-ui.example.com

input.command:
kubectl -n cockroachdb create secret tls crdb-ui-tls \
  --key /tmp/crdb-ui.key --cert /tmp/crdb-ui.crt 2>&1
input.description: Create TLS secret in cockroachdb namespace

content.content: secret/crdb-ui-tls created

tool_use_result.stdout: secret/crdb-ui-tls created

input.command:
kubectl apply -f - 2>&1 <<'EOF'
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: crdb-ui
  namespace: cockroachdb
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/ssl-redirect: "true"
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx
  tls:
  - hosts:
    - crdb-ui.example.com
    secretName: crdb-ui-tls
  rules:
  - host: crdb-ui.example.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: crdb-cluster-public
            port:
              number: 8080
EOF
input.description: Create Ingress resource for DB Console with TLS

input.command:
kubectl apply -f - 2>&1 <<'EOF'
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: crdb-ui
  namespace: cockroachdb
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/ssl-redirect: "true"
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx
  tls:
  - hosts:
    - crdb-ui.example.com
    secretName: crdb-ui-tls
  rules:
  - host: crdb-ui.example.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: crdb-cluster-public
            port:
              number: 8080
EOF

content.thinking:
The controller picked up the TCP services ConfigMap and the TLS secret. The warning about "local SSL certificate cockroachdb/crdb-ui-tls was not found" is normal during initial sync — it then found and added it ("Adding secret to local store").

Let me do a quick connectivity test to verify:
1. HTTPS DB Console: curl to port 443 with Host header crdb-ui.example.com
2. SQL on 26257: try to connect via the ingress-nginx service IP

The ingress-nginx service has ClusterIP 10.96.171.40 with port 443 mapped to nodePort 31009 and port 26257 mapped to nodePort 31740.
I'll run the connectivity checks from inside the cluster to see if the routing is working properly.

content.text: The controller picked up both the TCP ConfigMap and TLS secret. Let me do a quick connectivity test from within the cluster:

input.command:
NGINX_IP=$(kubectl -n ingress-nginx get svc ingress-nginx-controller -o jsonpath='{.spec.clusterIP}')
echo "Nginx ClusterIP: $NGINX_IP"

# Test HTTPS DB Console
kubectl -n cockroachdb exec curl-test -- curl -sk -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" \
  --resolve "crdb-ui.example.com:443:$NGINX_IP" \
  "https://crdb-ui.example.com/" 2>&1

echo ""

# Test TCP SQL port
kubectl -n cockroachdb exec curl-test -- sh -c \
  "echo '' | nc -w 3 $NGINX_IP 26257 2>&1 | head -c 100 | cat -v" 2>&1
input.description: Test HTTPS DB Console and TCP SQL connectivity through ingress-nginx

content.thinking:
The HTTPS DB Console returns HTTP 200 - great! 

The nc test might not have produced output because it exited before getting a response. Let me check if nc is available in the curl-test pod, or try a different approach.

Actually, since CockroachDB's SQL port speaks PostgreSQL wire protocol, the nc connection would output some binary/text data. The "no output" might mean that nc isn't available or the connection timed out.

Let me try with cockroach SQL client or check if the port is accessible.

input.command:
NGINX_IP=$(kubectl -n ingress-nginx get svc ingress-nginx-controller -o jsonpath='{.spec.clusterIP}')

# CockroachDB speaks PostgreSQL wire protocol - it sends a banner on connect
kubectl -n cockroachdb exec crdb-cluster-0 -- sh -c \
  "cockroach sql --insecure --host=${NGINX_IP} --port=26257 -e 'SELECT 1' 2>&1" || true

The controller IS listening on port 26257 (`0.0.0.0:26257` and `:::26257`). The cockroach SQL connection timed out earlier - let me try a more direct test. Maybe it was the `cockroach sql` client that has issues. Let me try with a direct TCP connection test using curl or a simpler tool.

Actually, wait - the `cockroach sql --insecure` test tried to connect to the nginx ClusterIP directly - that should work. Let me think about why it failed...

Actually, maybe the cockroach client binary can't connect because of how it sends the initial connection. 

CockroachDB uses the PostgreSQL wire protocol, so the "context deadline exceeded" error suggests the TCP connection might have established but the handshake didn't complete. Let me try a simpler connection test directly within the cluster to see if it's a client-side issue or something with the server's protocol handling.

content.text: Port 26257 is listening. Let me retry the SQL connection now that the controller has fully initialized:

input.command:
NGINX_IP=$(kubectl -n ingress-nginx get svc ingress-nginx-controller -o jsonpath='{.spec.clusterIP}')

kubectl -n cockroachdb exec crdb-cluster-0 -- \
  cockroach sql --insecure --host=${NGINX_IP} --port=26257 \
  --timeout=10s -e 'SELECT 1 AS test' 2>&1

content.thinking:
The nginx controller IS seeing TCP connections on port 26257 and trying to forward them to the upstream (CockroachDB pods' IPs), but the upstream is timing out. 

The upstream IPs (10.244.0.120, 10.244.0.117) look like pod IPs. But wait - the service is `crdb-cluster-public` which is a ClusterIP service pointing to the pods. The nginx ingress controller should be forwarding to the service's ClusterIP (10.96.114.215), not the pod IPs directly.
Actually, nginx resolving to pod IPs directly via endpoints is normal behavior. The real issue is that nginx can reach the pod IPs but the connection times out when trying to establish the TCP connection to port 26257 - either the CockroachDB pods are rejecting external connections, there's a network policy blocking it, or a firewall is dropping the packets.

content.thinking:
The NetworkPolicy `block-crdb-sql` currently only allows inbound traffic to port 8080 (DB Console) on the CockroachDB pods. It blocks all other ingress, including port 26257 (SQL).

I need to add a rule to allow traffic on port 26257 from the ingress-nginx namespace. But I need to be careful — the task says to expose SQL via ingress-nginx on TCP port 26257. The NetworkPolicy is blocking direct pod-to-pod traffic, but the nginx controller needs to be able to forward to the CRDB pods on port 26257.
Looking at the current policy more closely, the ingress rule for port 8080 has no `from` selector, so it allows traffic from any source. Port 26257 isn't covered at all, which means it's completely blocked. I need to patch the NetworkPolicy to add a rule that allows port 26257 specifically from the ingress-nginx namespace.

Patching the NetworkPolicy...

content.text: The NetworkPolicy only allows port 8080 and blocks port 26257. I need to add an ingress rule for SQL from the ingress-nginx namespace:

content.text: SQL is now working. Let me do a final verification of both endpoints:

input.command:
NGINX_IP=$(kubectl -n ingress-nginx get svc ingress-nginx-controller -o jsonpath='{.spec.clusterIP}')

echo "=== HTTPS DB Console ==="
kubectl -n cockroachdb exec curl-test -- curl -sk -o /dev/null -w "HTTP %{http_code}\n" \
  --resolve "crdb-ui.example.com:443:${NGINX_IP}" \
  "https://crdb-ui.example.com/"

echo ""
echo "=== SQL via TCP port 26257 ==="
kubectl -n cockroachdb exec crdb-cluster-0 -- \
  cockroach sql --insecure --host=${NGINX_IP} --port=26257 \
  -e 'SELECT version() AS crdb_version' 2>&1
input.description: Final verification of both HTTPS DB Console and SQL TCP endpoints
